attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of increasing pain and swelling in his left knee. He can no longer weight-bear. He had a cycle race five days ago and does not remember hurting his knee. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He consumes two glasses of wine a day.

On examination, he appears unwell. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 105/65 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.5°C. There is a large, tender and warm effusion around the left knee with a limited range of movement. There are no other abnormal findings. A plain X-ray of the knee shows the swelling, with chondrocalcinosis, and no fracture. Synovial fluid aspiration shows:

  • Leucocytes: 110,000/mm³ (98% neutrophils)

  • Occasional intracellular rhomboid-shaped crystals, showing some birefringence

  • Gram stain: ++ (gram-positive cocci in clusters)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the definitive management for this condition?
attributes.explanation:
This patient most likely has septic arthritis, given the acute onset of monoarticular joint pain, with a red, swollen and warm joint. In addition, chondrocalcinosis (as seen on the X-ray) and rhomboid-shaped positively birefringent crystals (as seen in synovial fluid) are suggestive of an underlying pseudogout. Underlying joint disease is a risk factor for the development of septic arthritis. A leucocyte count of over 100,000 and Gram positive cocci in clusters are very suggestive of an infective cause for the arthritis (though such levels of leukocytes can sometimes occur in non-infective causes).

Arthroscopic washout/debridement is the definitive management for septic arthritis of the knee. This procedure provides source control, which is crucial in managing any severe infection. It allows for thorough irrigation of the joint, removal of infected synovial tissue, and drainage of purulent material. Even if the diagnosis of septic arthritis is eventually discounted (i.e., if joint fluid cultures are negative), it is safer to presume a septic cause and treat as such, rather than delaying treatment and risking joint destruction. Arthroscopic washout also provides an additional opportunity to obtain synovial biopsies for further microbiological diagnosis.

Intravenous flucloxacillin and vancomycin is indeed the correct antibiotic combination in this scenario, as the most common causative organism in septic arthritis is Staphylococcus aureus. However, while antibiotic therapy is an important part of treatment, it is not the definitive management. Without surgical intervention, antibiotics alone are unlikely to resolve septic arthritis effectively.

Intravenous ceftriaxone would be appropriate if a gram-negative infection was suspected. It will cover most Gram-negative organisms and streptococcal species. However, given the Gram stain shows Gram-positive cocci in clusters, which is classic for Staphylococcus aureus, this would not be the most appropriate choice.

Colchicine is used in the treatment of gout and pseudogout but would not be appropriate as the definitive treatment for septic arthritis. Similarly, intra-articular prednisolone might be considered for non-infectious inflammatory arthritis but is contraindicated in septic arthritis.

In summary, while antibiotic therapy and management of underlying conditions are important, the definitive management for septic arthritis of the knee is arthroscopic washout/debridement. This surgical intervention provides the necessary source control and is crucial for preventing joint destruction and achieving the best possible outcome.
learningPoints: Arthroscopic washout is the definitive treatment for septic arthritis of the knee.
Source control through surgical intervention is the most important aspect of management in septic arthritis.
Empiric antibiotic therapy should cover common causative organisms, including MRSA in high-risk patients.
references: Hassan AS, Rao A, Manadan AM, et al. Peripheral Bacterial Septic Arthritis. J Clin Rheum 2017; 23:435-42. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/28926460/
